input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **BIM-based infrastructure asset** within the **AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain**, focusing on **building life cycle management**. The system comprises **semantically rich, object-based 3D models** generated via BIM, incorporating **geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics** of building components (e.g., structural elements, MEP systems, façade panels) and their **interrelationships** (e.g., spatial adjacency, performance dependencies). Key characteristics include:
  - **Policy-driven adoption**: Mandated by regulatory initiatives and industry standards (e.g., [60]) to ensure standardized digital representation.
  - **Collaborative framework**: Enables multi-disciplinary coordination (architects, engineers, construction professionals) for **clash detection**, **performance simulation**, and **documentation generation**.
  - **Lifecycle-centric**: Supports **design-to-operations** continuity, aligning with **operational control** requirements for infrastructure assets.
  - **Interoperability**: Designed to integrate with **industry-specific solutions** (e.g., Model-Based Systems Engineering in manufacturing) while prioritizing **BIM as the standard paradigm** for construction.
  The system’s suitability for digital twinning stems from its **semantic richness**, **policy-backed standardization**, and **operational relevance** across the building lifecycle phases.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** system provides the following **service types** to users (e.g., architects, engineers, construction managers, facility operators) and the **physical system**, grounded in **model-driven automation** and **real-time connectivity**:
  - **Monitoring Services**:
    - **Real-time synchronization** between physical assets and digital models via **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto**, enabling live data ingestion (e.g., sensor telemetry, environmental conditions, structural health metrics).
    - **Continuous state tracking** of building components (e.g., temperature, humidity, vibration, occupancy) for **condition monitoring**.
  - **Optimization Services**:
    - **Performance-driven decision support** leveraging BIM models to optimize **energy efficiency**, **resource allocation**, and **maintenance scheduling** (e.g., predictive maintenance alerts).
    - **Clash resolution assistance** during design phases using **semantic conflict detection** in 3D models.
  - **Prediction Services**:
    - **Lifecycle forecasting** for building components (e.g., degradation modeling, remaining useful life estimation) based on **real-time and historical data**.
    - **Scenario simulation** for **what-if analysis** (e.g., impact of design changes, operational adjustments on system performance).
  - **Control Services**:
    - **Automated deployment** of operational control strategies (e.g., HVAC optimization, lighting management) via **model-driven automation frameworks**.
    - **Closed-loop feedback** between DT and physical systems for **adaptive control** (e.g., dynamic response to environmental changes).
  - **Visualization Services**:
    - **Immersive 3D/4D/5D modeling** (geometric + temporal + cost) for **collaborative review** and **stakeholder communication**.
    - **Real-time dashboarding** with **interactive analytics** (e.g., heatmaps, trend visualizations) for operational oversight.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Design-phase**: Architects, engineers (for clash detection, performance simulation).
    - **Construction-phase**: Project managers, contractors (for progress tracking, resource optimization).
    - **Operations-phase**: Facility managers, maintenance teams (for predictive maintenance, energy management).
  The services are **domain-specific** to AECO, emphasizing **scalability**, **interoperability**, and **semantic integration** to bridge **design-time models** and **runtime operations**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin system** is implemented using a **technical stack** and **methodological enablers** aligned with **Design Science Research (DSR)** principles, focusing on **model-driven deployment** and **operational control**. Key components include:
  - **Software Platforms/Frameworks**:
    - **Eclipse Hono**: A **real-time data integration platform** enabling **secure, scalable IoT data ingestion** from physical assets (e.g., sensors, actuators) into the DT. Facilitates **protocol-agnostic connectivity** (e.g., MQTT, CoAP) for heterogeneous device ecosystems.
    - **Eclipse Ditto**: A **digital twin framework** providing **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital representations, with **event-driven state management** and **versioning support** for model consistency.
    - **Model-Driven Automation Framework**: A **DSR-derived artifact** that automates DT deployment via **BIM-based templates**, reducing manual intervention (as per **R2**). Supports **reproducible setups** for operational control DTs.
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **Modular Architecture**: Designed with **defined extension points** (per **R4**) to enable **service-oriented development** in AECO. Supports **plug-and-play integration** of third-party tools (e.g., simulation engines, analytics platforms).
    - **Design Science Methodology**: Structured around **requirements elicitation** (Section III-A) and **prototyping/experimentation** (Section V), ensuring **iterative refinement** of DT artifacts.
  - **Simulation Engines/Databases**:
    - **BIM Software Suite**: Leverages **semantically enriched 3D models** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) as the **source-of-truth** for geometric and functional data. Integrates with **performance simulation tools** (e.g., EnergyPlus, Navisworks) for **predictive analytics**.
    - **No explicit database mentioned**, but **real-time synchronization** (via Hono/Ditto) implies **low-latency storage solutions** (e.g., time-series databases, graph databases) for **asset telemetry** and **model state tracking**.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Semantic Integration Layer**: Enables **ontology-driven alignment** between BIM models and runtime data (e.g., linking IFC schemas to IoT sensor data) for **unified semantics**.
    - **AECO-Specific Protocols**: Uses **industry standards** (e.g., **Industry Foundation Classes (IFC)**) for **interoperability** across BIM tools and DT platforms.
    - **Automation Enablers**: **R2** highlights **automated deployment processes** (e.g., CI/CD pipelines for DT templates) to ensure **reproducibility** and **scalability**.
  - **Technical Specifications**:
    - **Real-time connectivity**: Achieved via **Eclipse Hono/Ditto** with **sub-second latency** for data synchronization (implied by **R3**).
    - **Scalability**: Enabled by **modular, service-oriented architecture** (per **R4**), allowing **horizontal scaling** of DT services.
    - **Extensibility**: Supported by **well-defined APIs** and **plugin architectures** for custom service integration.
  The tooling is **tailored to AECO**, prioritizing **BIM compatibility**, **real-time operations**, and **domain-specific automation** to operationalize DTs for infrastructure assets.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system supports **real-time data exchange capabilities** between physical and digital components, ensuring continuous synchronization with **low-latency requirements** to maintain operational fidelity. The framework enables **dynamic operational monitoring and control** at fine temporal granularity, aligning with the **runtime configuration management** for adaptable adjustments without full redeployment. While explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz or ms intervals) are not specified, the emphasis on **immersive interactive visualization** and **bidirectional data exchange** implies sub-second or near-real-time updates for critical operational services. The system prioritizes **temporal consistency** between enriched BIM models and operational DTs, leveraging modular architecture to accommodate varying time scales for different services (e.g., device-level monitoring vs. facility-wide analytics).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin framework adopts a **modular, hierarchical architecture** with **centralized coordination** via a central hub (DT Framework) that facilitates bidirectional data exchange. **Multiple Digital Twin instances** can be deployed per building or infrastructure component, with **scalability** from individual components to entire systems. **Decentralized extensions** are supported through standardized extension points, allowing **DT Engineers** to integrate custom services while maintaining centralized oversight. Responsibilities are distributed among roles: **Building Control Engineers** manage device modeling and deployment, **Facility Managers** interact with deployed twins for real-time monitoring, and **DT Engineers** extend capabilities. Coordination mechanisms rely on **runtime configuration management** to enable **low-overhead updates** without full redeployment, ensuring consistency across instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows a **centralized orchestration pattern** with a **hub-and-spoke architecture**, where the **DT Framework** acts as the central hub coordinating roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and system components. **Component integration** is achieved via **modular design** with standardized extension points, enabling seamless addition of custom services and analytics. **Resource management** is implicit in the framework’s **scalability** requirements, ensuring efficient allocation across heterogeneous systems. System-wide coordination relies on **bidirectional data exchange**, **runtime configuration management**, and **interactive visualization interfaces** to maintain operational coherence. The framework emphasizes **holistic building life cycle management**, integrating enriched BIM models with operational DTs through coordinated roles and dynamic service extension.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system emphasizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** through adherence to **non-functional requirements (NF_X)** addressing **interoperability standards** and **data exchange protocols**. While specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, IoT protocols) are not mentioned, the framework supports **integration with external systems** via standardized extension points and modular architecture. **Dependencies on external systems** are managed through **low-overhead updates** and **runtime configuration**, ensuring seamless interaction with BIM tools, facility management systems, and enterprise infrastructure. The system also implies **integration patterns** with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems via the **centralized hub’s bidirectional data exchange**, enabling cross-system coordination for **building life cycle management** in the AECO domain.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system employs a **model-driven deployment framework** that integrates **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** as the foundational data source, augmented to address BIM’s limitations in representing Building Control Systems (BCS) components. The framework supports **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** through enriched BIM representations, where **Building Control Engineers** define **sensors, actuators, and controllers** directly within the BIM environment. This creates a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations via **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles**, enabling automated extraction of structured representations from enriched BIM models.

Data components include:
- **Model types**: Geometric (BIM-based spatial representations), behavioral (control logic and device configurations), and analytical (simulation models for performance prediction).
- **Data sources**: Primarily BIM models (with BCS extensions), complemented by real-time data feeds from physical sensors and actuators.
- **Data management approaches**: Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) for automated artifact generation, standardized extension points for modularity, and real-time synchronization mechanisms to maintain alignment between physical and digital twins.
- **Model relationships**: Hierarchical and dependency-based relationships between BIM components, control logic, and runtime services, facilitated by a **modular architecture** with standardized interfaces for custom services.

The framework leverages **automated transformation pipelines** to generate deployable DT artifacts (e.g., runtime services, visualization interfaces) from enriched BIM models, minimizing manual intervention while ensuring consistency across the DT constellation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The physical acting components are defined and configured by **Building Control Engineers** within the BIM environment, enabling direct integration of **actuators and controllers** into the Digital Twin system. The framework supports **remote control interfaces** for Facility Managers to operate physical infrastructure via the Digital Twin, though specific **types of actuators**, **actuation ranges**, or **safety constraints** are not detailed in the provided documents.

Key aspects inferred from the context:
- **Control capabilities**: Implemented via **control logic** defined in the BIM environment, enabling automated or manual actuation based on real-time data.
- **Remote control interfaces**: Facilitate interaction between Facility Managers and the Digital Twin for operational oversight, though the underlying protocols or communication standards (e.g., OPC UA, Modbus) are not specified.
- **Safety constraints**: Implicitly addressed through the framework’s **modular architecture**, which allows for integration of custom safety services or plugins by **DT Engineers**, though explicit constraints (e.g., fail-safes, rate limits) are not documented.

The sensing infrastructure is implicitly defined through the **BIM-enriched models** created by Building Control Engineers, which include **sensors** as configurable components. However, the provided documents do **not explicitly detail** the following critical specifications:
- **Sensor types** (e.g., temperature, humidity, motion, structural strain sensors),
- **Measurement capabilities** (e.g., resolution, dynamic range),
- **Spatial distribution** (e.g., sensor density, coverage areas),
- **Sampling frequencies** or **data transmission rates**,
- **Accuracy and precision specifications**,
- **Data collection mechanisms** (e.g., wired vs. wireless, protocols like LoRaWAN, Zigbee).

The framework assumes sensors are integrated into the BIM environment as part of the **device modeling phase**, but no technical specifications or performance metrics are provided beyond their role in enabling **real-time data exchange** for synchronization between the physical and digital twins.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The framework addresses **model fidelity requirements** and **validation/verification (V&V) processes** through the following mechanisms:
- **Fidelity requirements**: Achieved via **enriched BIM models** that extend static representations to include dynamic BCS components (sensors, actuators, controllers), ensuring the Digital Twin reflects the **complete life cycle** of the physical system. The **model-driven approach** minimizes discrepancies by automating transformations from BIM to deployable artifacts.
- **Validation and verification methods**: Implicitly supported by the **modular architecture**, which allows for **custom services and plugins** developed by DT Engineers to validate data integrity and model accuracy. However, explicit methods (e.g., statistical testing, comparative analysis) are not detailed.
- **Uncertainty sources and quantification**: Not explicitly addressed in the documents, though the framework’s **real-time synchronization** aims to mitigate temporal discrepancies between physical and digital states.
- **Error detection and correction mechanisms**: Likely handled through **custom services** integrated by DT Engineers, though no specific algorithms or protocols (e.g., Kalman filtering, anomaly detection) are mentioned.
- **Quality assurance processes**: Relied upon the **standardized extension points** and **automated artifact generation** to ensure consistency, but no formal QA methodologies (e.g., ISO standards, audits) are referenced.

extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain spans multiple phases, including **conceptualization, design, construction, operation, and decommissioning**, with each phase characterized by distinct representation types and transition management challenges. During **conceptualization**, DTs are initialized with **BIM (Building Information Modeling) models** as foundational digital representations, often augmented with **sensor networks** for real-time data capture. The **design phase** emphasizes **modular construction workflows**, where DTs integrate **AI-driven simulations** to optimize structural and operational parameters, with transitions managed via **version-controlled BIM schemas** (e.g., IFC, COBie). **Construction** leverages DTs for **real-time synchronization** between physical assets and digital models, facilitated by **IoT-enabled sensors** and **automated workflows** (e.g., progress tracking via BIM 360 or Navisworks). The **operation phase** focuses on **life cycle management**, where DTs enable predictive maintenance, energy optimization, and cost modeling through **bidirectional communication protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) and **AI/ML analytics**. Transition governance is hindered by **fragmented, case-specific deployments** lacking standardized schemas, necessitating **unified engineering workflows** and **stakeholder alignment** across phases. Lifecycle governance frameworks (e.g., ISO 55000 for asset management) are often **implicitly referenced** but not uniformly adopted, leading to gaps in interoperability and scalability.

Key technical enablers include:
- **BIM-centric representations** (IFC, COBie) for design/construction phases.
- **Sensor networks + IoT** for real-time data synchronization during operation.
- **AI/ML integration** for predictive analytics and decision support.
- **Version control** via BIM tools (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) with limited automation.
- **Transition barriers**: Lack of standardized data schemas, bidirectional protocols, and governance models across phases.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process in AECO DT systems follows a **methodology-driven, iterative approach** with **limited automation** and **manual configuration** as primary constraints. Development begins with **BIM model integration**, where physical assets are mapped to digital twins using **proprietary or semi-standardized schemas** (e.g., IFC for geometry, COBie for asset data). **Quality assurance** relies on **case-specific validation protocols**, often lacking formalized testing frameworks beyond basic interoperability checks (e.g., IFC compliance). **Version control** is managed via BIM tools (e.g., Revit’s collaboration tools) or proprietary platforms (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin), but **scalable automation** is restricted due to **fragmented workflows** and **high manual effort** in integrating analytics and orchestration components.

Evolution strategies include:
- **Incremental updates** via sensor data feeds and AI-driven model refinements (e.g., machine learning for defect detection).
- **Modular construction focus**: DTs are adapted for **prefabricated components** with **pre-validated digital twins** to reduce on-site integration complexity.
- **Orchestration challenges**: Frameworks like **Eclipse Ditto** or **BaSyx** (industrial IoT-focused) require **manual configuration** for AECO-specific use cases, with **low out-of-the-box functionality** for non-developers.
- **Testing and validation**: Primarily **ad-hoc**, relying on **stakeholder-driven reviews** rather than automated regression testing or compliance checks.
- **Evolution barriers**: Absence of **unified engineering workflows**, **high implementation costs**, and **proprietary ecosystem dependencies** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js requiring substantial manual effort).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT system in AECO generates **real-time and predictive insights** through **AI/ML analytics**, **sensor data processing**, and **simulation-based modeling**, but decision support capabilities are **fragmented and user-dependent**. Key insights include:
- **Operational efficiency metrics** (e.g., energy consumption, equipment health) derived from **IoT sensor networks** and **BIM-integrated DTs**.
- **Predictive maintenance alerts** via **AI-driven anomaly detection** in operational data streams.
- **Cost modeling** for life cycle phases, enabled by **simulation tools** (e.g., energy performance analysis in Revit or Grasshopper).
- **Decision-making processes** are **stakeholder-driven**, with **limited automated recommendation engines** due to **lack of standardized analytics pipelines**. User interaction occurs via **proprietary platforms** (e.g., iTwin’s visualization tools) or **custom dashboards**, often requiring **manual data interpretation**.

Confidence measures for recommendations are **implicit**, relying on:
- **Sensor accuracy** (not quantified in documents).
- **AI model performance** (e.g., defect detection accuracy, not specified).
- **Stakeholder validation** (no formal confidence scoring frameworks mentioned).

User interaction methods include:
- **Visual analytics** (3D BIM/DT overlays for spatial decision-making).
- **Alert-based notifications** (e.g., maintenance warnings via IoT gateways).
- **Limited automation**: Decisions often require **human-in-the-loop** due to **lack of unified governance models**.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
Standardization in AECO DT systems is **fragmented**, with **partial compliance** to industry standards but **no unified frameworks** for interoperability or governance. Key standards and specifications include:
- **BIM-related**: IFC (Industry Foundation Classes) for **geometry and asset data exchange**, COBie (Construction Operations Building Information Exchange) for **asset handover documentation**.
- **Connectivity protocols**: OPC UA and MQTT for **bidirectional communication** between physical assets and DTs, though adoption is **case-specific**.
- **Industrial IoT frameworks**: Eclipse Ditto and BaSyx provide **standard compliance** (e.g., OPC UA, AMQP) but are **not AECO-optimized**, requiring **manual integration** for AECO-specific use cases.
- **Certification requirements**: None explicitly mentioned for DT systems; compliance is **implicit** via BIM tool certifications (e.g., Autodesk Revit’s IFC compliance).

Interoperability challenges:
- **Lack of standardized data schemas** across DT lifecycle phases.
- **Proprietary ecosystem dependencies** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js, Autodesk’s Forge) limit cross-platform compatibility.
- **No unified governance models**: Standards like ISO 55000 (asset management) are **referenced but not enforced** in DT implementations.
- **Open-source frameworks** (e.g., Gil et al.’s 14 identified frameworks) are **IoT/industrial-focused**, lacking AECO-specific **BIM integration** or **workflow automation**.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: The provided documents do not explicitly detail data governance policies, ownership frameworks, or privacy protection measures (e.g., GDPR compliance, consent management protocols, or anonymization techniques) for Digital Twin systems in AECO domains. While the studies by Gerber et al. [33] and Opoku et al. [34] discuss challenges in data integration and stakeholder coordination—including potential conflicts arising from multi-party data sharing—they do not specify technical implementations for data ownership, access controls, or regulatory compliance. No mention of proprietary or standardized data-sharing agreements, encryption standards, or audit trails is present in the surveyed works. Thus, **Not in Document**.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The documents highlight critical security and safety considerations in Digital Twin systems, particularly in AECO contexts, though they lack explicit technical protocols. Gerber et al. [33] and Opoku et al. [34] emphasize **real-time data utilization** and **3D visualization** for facility management, implicitly requiring robust cybersecurity to prevent unauthorized access or manipulation of IoT/BIM-integrated data streams. Abbasi et al. [49] address **semantic drift**—a form of model-physical divergence—that could expose vulnerabilities if not mitigated, necessitating **synchronization techniques** and **runtime adaptability** (as per Lehner et al. [48]) to maintain system integrity. However, no specific mention of **access control mechanisms** (e.g., role-based access control), **authentication systems** (e.g., OAuth 2.0, blockchain-based identity), **threat mitigation strategies** (e.g., anomaly detection in data streams), or **fail-safe mechanisms** (e.g., redundancy protocols) is provided. The focus remains conceptual, with no technical specifications for encryption, intrusion detection, or compliance with safety-critical standards (e.g., ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53). Thus, **Not in Document**.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],
block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime
block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
